title: Continuous Pretraining
concept: Continuous pretraining takes a general model and trains it further on domain text before fine-tuning, adapting its representations without starting from scratch.

exercises.prompt: What does continuous pretraining mean to "continuously pretrain" a model mean?

options.text: Training an existing model further on domain text
Training a model from scratch with random weights
Deleting the model's memory to start over

exercises.explain: Continuous pretraining takes an already trained model and feeds it more domain-specific text to adapt its representations.

cards.title: Upgrading an Already Smart Brain
cards.body: Ever wondered how a general-purpose AI learns to speak like a specialized lawyer or a clinical doctor? Instead of teaching it from scratch, we give its brain a quick boost of target domain reading. Let's see how!

cards.title: What is Continuous Pretraining?
cards.body: Continuous pretraining is the process of taking an already trained general AI model and training it further on a large volume of unlabeled, domain-specific text (like medical journals or legal documents) to teach it specialized language before fine-tuning it for specific tasks.

cards.title: The Doctor's Specialization
cards.body: Think of continuous pretraining like a general doctor who decides to become a cardiologist. Instead of going back to elementary school (starting from scratch), they go to medical school for specialized heart training. They build directly on top of the general knowledge they already have!

cards.title: Learning Wall Street Lingo
cards.body: Imagine taking a standard LLM and feeding it thousands of textbooks on financial markets and stock trading rules. By doing this continuous pretraining, the model naturally learns terms like "liquidity" and "short-selling" in context before it is trained to write financial reports.

cards.title: Does it Forget Everything?
cards.body: Some think continuous pretraining completely erases what the model already knows. In fact, the model retains its basic grammar, reasoning, and world knowledge, but refines its understanding of domain-specific vocabulary and concepts.

cards.title: Scratch vs. Continuous
cards.body: See how continuous pretraining compares to starting completely from scratch (from-scratch training).

left.text: Starts with random weights and requires learning basic grammar, vocabulary, and facts from zero.

right.text: Starts with a fully capable model and updates it to learn specialized vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: What is a major advantage of using continuous pretraining over training a new model from scratch?

options.text: It saves massive amounts of time and computing power.
It makes the model forget how to speak English.
It guarantees the model will never make a mistake.
It requires manual human labels for every single sentence.

exercises.explain: Continuous pretraining saves enormous amounts of time and electricity because we start with a model that already understands basic human language, rather than starting from scratch.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Continuous pretraining is typically done before fine-tuning the model for a specific task.
exercises.explain: Continuous pretraining is done first to help the model learn the general language of the domain, followed by fine-tuning to teach it how to perform specific tasks.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the description of continuous pretraining data.
exercises.sentence: Continuous pretraining adapts a model by training it further on massive amounts of _ text.
exercises.explain: We use unlabeled text for continuous pretraining because we want the model to learn the patterns of the domain language naturally, just like it did during its initial pretraining.

exercises.prompt: Which response is more likely generated by a model that has gone through continuous pretraining on professional medical literature?

options.text: "The patient had a bad pain in their chest, and we need to check their heart pumps."
"The patient presented with acute angina; scheduled an urgent echocardiogram."
"My chest hurts, can you please call a doctor right away?"

exercises.explain: The second text shows a model that has undergone continuous pretraining on medical texts, allowing it to correctly identify clinical terms instead of using generic language.

exercises.prompt: Your team has a general language model and wants to adapt it for the medical field. Where does continuous pretraining fit into your pipeline?

options.text: It replaces the original general knowledge entirely.
It adapts general knowledge to domain text before fine-tuning.
It is only done after the model is fully fine-tuned.
It trains the model from scratch using random weights.

exercises.explain: Continuous pretraining sits right in the middle: it updates the model's base knowledge using massive unlabeled domain text before you do the final supervised fine-tuning for specific tasks.
